items.title: Declaration of Independence
items.author: Thomas Jefferson (primary)
items.byline: Asserts natural rights and the people's right to alter or abolish a government that violates them
items.key_takeaway: Governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed, and when they violate natural rights, the people may alter or abolish them.
items.summary: Drawing on John Locke's social contract theory, the Declaration argues that all people possess unalienable natural rights — life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness — and that governments exist to secure these rights. When a government becomes destructive of those rights, the governed have the right to reform or replace it. The document lists specific grievances against King George III as evidence that the colonies were justified in breaking from Britain. It establishes the philosophical foundation that legitimate government requires popular consent.
items.exam_appearance: Argument Essay prompts on legitimacy of government · MCQs on social contract theory

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on the Declaration of Independence. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time, wait for my answer, then tell me if I'm right and explain why before moving to the next.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain the Declaration of Independence in more depth. Cover John Locke's influence, the concept of natural rights, the social contract, and how the Declaration's arguments compare to Federalist 10 and Brutus 1.

items.title: Articles of Confederation
items.author: Second Continental Congress
items.byline: First US constitution — so weak it was replaced within a decade
items.key_takeaway: The Articles' weak central government — no power to tax, regulate commerce, or enforce laws — proved the need for a stronger federal system under the Constitution.
items.summary: The Articles created a loose confederation of sovereign states with a single-chamber Congress that lacked the power to tax, regulate interstate or foreign commerce, raise an army, or enforce its own laws. Amendments required unanimous consent of all 13 states, making reform nearly impossible. Shays' Rebellion (1786) exposed the federal government's inability to maintain order and pushed the states to convene the Constitutional Convention in 1787. The failures of the Articles are the exam's go-to example of why a balance between state and national power matters.

paired_with.title: Federalist No. 10
paired_with.relation: argues for the stronger union it creates

items.exam_appearance: FRQ comparisons of federalism structures · MCQs on historical context of the Constitution

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on the Articles of Confederation. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time, wait for my answer, then tell me if I'm right and explain why before moving to the next.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain the Articles of Confederation in more depth. Cover its specific weaknesses, Shays' Rebellion, and how each weakness was directly addressed in the US Constitution.

items.title: US Constitution
items.author: Framers (Constitutional Convention)
items.byline: Supreme law of the land — structure, enumerated powers, and amendments
items.key_takeaway: The Constitution establishes a federal system with separation of powers, checks and balances, and a written framework that can be amended but remains the supreme law under the Supremacy Clause.
items.summary: The Constitution creates three branches with distinct powers — legislative (Article I), executive (Article II), judicial (Article III) — and builds in checks and balances so no branch dominates. Federalism divides authority between the national government (enumerated and implied powers) and the states (reserved powers under the 10th Amendment). The Supremacy Clause (Article VI) makes federal law supreme when it conflicts with state law. Amendment requires a supermajority in Congress and ratification by three-fourths of states, making change deliberate but difficult. The Bill of Rights (first 10 amendments) was added in 1791 to address Anti-Federalist concerns.

paired_with.title: McCulloch v. Maryland
paired_with.relation: interprets its Necessary and Proper Clause

items.exam_appearance: SCOTUS Comparison FRQs cite specific clauses · Argument Essay prompts invoke its values · foundational to nearly every MCQ stimulus

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on the US Constitution. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time covering structure, separation of powers, federalism, and the amendment process. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain the US Constitution in more depth. Cover the structure of each article, how checks and balances work between the three branches, and the distinction between enumerated, implied, and reserved powers.

items.title: Federalist No. 10
items.author: James Madison
items.byline: Argues a large republic is the best defense against tyranny of faction
items.key_takeaway: A large republic with many competing factions makes tyranny of the majority less likely than a small direct democracy would.
items.summary: Madison defines factions as groups united by a common interest adverse to the rights of others or the public good. Removing the causes of faction would either destroy liberty or require everyone to think the same — both unacceptable. The solution is to control the effects of faction through a large republic that (a) elects representatives who refine public views and (b) makes it structurally hard for any single faction to form a national majority. More factions competing means no one group can dominate. This is the foundational pluralist argument for representative democracy over direct democracy.
items.constitutional_link: Article I · structure of Congress and representation
items.exam_appearance: Argument Essay prompts on factions and pluralism · stimulus-based MCQs with document excerpts

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Federalist No. 10. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about factions, the large republic solution, and Madison's argument. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Federalist No. 10 in more depth. Cover Madison's definition of faction, why removing causes of faction is impossible, why a large republic controls faction effects, and how it compares to Brutus 1's opposing view.

items.title: Brutus No. 1
items.author: Brutus (likely Robert Yates, Anti-Federalist)
items.byline: Anti-Federalist warning that a strong central government will destroy state sovereignty
items.key_takeaway: A large republic with a powerful central government, elastic clause, and supremacy clause will inevitably consolidate power and threaten individual liberties — which is why a Bill of Rights is essential.
items.summary: Brutus argues the proposed Constitution grants too much power to a distant central government. The Necessary and Proper Clause (elastic clause) and the Supremacy Clause would let Congress expand its authority indefinitely at the expense of state sovereignty. A republic over a territory as large as the United States cannot truly represent its citizens — representatives will be too far removed from constituents, and competing interests too diverse to reconcile. Brutus insists only small republics can preserve liberty. This is the core Anti-Federalist argument and the intellectual foundation for demanding a Bill of Rights.
items.constitutional_link: Article I Section 8 (Necessary and Proper) · Article VI (Supremacy)
items.exam_appearance: Argument Essay prompts paired with Federalist 10 · MCQs on Anti-Federalist concerns

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Brutus No. 1. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about Anti-Federalist concerns, the elastic clause, and the small-republic argument. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Brutus No. 1 in more depth. Cover the Anti-Federalist critique of central power, the concern with the Necessary and Proper and Supremacy clauses, and how it directly opposes Federalist 10's argument.

items.title: Federalist No. 51
items.author: James Madison
items.byline: Defends separation of powers and checks and balances as essential to controlling government
items.key_takeaway: Separation of powers and checks and balances are necessary because 'if men were angels, no government would be necessary' — ambition must be made to counteract ambition.
items.summary: Madison argues that each branch of government must be given the constitutional means and personal motives to resist encroachments by the others. Because people are not angels, government is necessary — but because those who govern are not angels either, government must be structured to control itself. Federalism adds a 'double security' to the rights of the people: power is first divided between national and state governments, then subdivided among three branches within each. The legislature is the most powerful branch and therefore splits into two chambers (bicameralism) to further check itself.
items.exam_appearance: FRQs on separation of powers and branch conflict · MCQs citing 'ambition counteracts ambition'

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Federalist No. 51. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about separation of powers, checks and balances, and bicameralism. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Federalist No. 51 in more depth. Cover Madison's 'if men were angels' argument, how the three branches check each other with specific examples, and the idea of double security through federalism.

items.title: Federalist No. 70
items.author: Alexander Hamilton
items.byline: Argues for a single, energetic executive as essential to good government
items.key_takeaway: A single executive — not a plural or council-based one — is necessary for the energy, decisiveness, and accountability a functional government requires.
items.summary: Hamilton argues that 'energy in the executive' is essential to protecting the nation, enforcing laws, securing property, and protecting liberty against domestic factions and foreign threats. He rejects a plural executive (multiple co-presidents) because it breeds delay, disagreement, and concealed responsibility. A single president ensures unity, decisive action, and — crucially — clear accountability: voters know exactly who is responsible when things go wrong. Energy requires unity, duration in office, adequate provision of support, and competent powers. This is the exam's canonical defense of a strong, unitary presidency.
items.exam_appearance: FRQs on presidential power and accountability · MCQs on energy in the executive

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Federalist No. 70. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about Hamilton's arguments for a single energetic executive. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Federalist No. 70 in more depth. Cover what Hamilton means by 'energy in the executive,' why he rejects a plural executive, and how accountability and unity work as arguments for the unitary presidency.

items.title: Federalist No. 78
items.author: Alexander Hamilton
items.byline: Defends an independent judiciary with lifetime appointments and the power of judicial review
items.key_takeaway: The judiciary is the 'least dangerous branch' because it controls neither sword nor purse — so it must be independent via lifetime appointments to protect rights and check legislative overreach.
items.summary: Hamilton defends lifetime tenure for federal judges as essential to judicial independence — only judges insulated from political pressure can impartially interpret the Constitution. The judiciary is the 'least dangerous' branch because it has no control over the military (sword) or the treasury (purse), only judgment. Hamilton anticipates and defends judicial review: when a law conflicts with the Constitution, courts must follow the Constitution because it is the higher law, the expression of the people's will. This argument was later affirmed in Marbury v. Madison (1803).
items.exam_appearance: FRQs on judicial independence · MCQs citing 'least dangerous branch' or lifetime tenure

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Federalist No. 78. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about judicial independence, lifetime tenure, and judicial review. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Federalist No. 78 in more depth. Cover why Hamilton calls the judiciary the 'least dangerous branch,' his defense of lifetime tenure, and how it anticipates judicial review as established in Marbury v. Madison.

items.title: Letter from Birmingham Jail
items.author: Martin Luther King Jr.
items.byline: Defends nonviolent civil disobedience and the moral obligation to disobey unjust laws
items.key_takeaway: Citizens have a moral responsibility to disobey unjust laws through nonviolent direct action, because 'injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere.'
items.summary: Written in jail after King was arrested for protesting segregation, the letter responds to white clergy who called his nonviolent direct action 'unwise and untimely.' King argues there are two types of laws: just laws that align with moral law and must be obeyed, and unjust laws that degrade human dignity and must be disobeyed — openly, lovingly, and with willingness to accept punishment. He rejects gradualism, defends the urgency of civil rights, and ties the movement to America's founding ideals of natural rights and equality. This is the exam's primary civil-rights-era document and a direct application of natural-rights philosophy.
items.exam_appearance: Argument Essay prompts on civil disobedience · MCQs pairing with civil rights cases

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Letter from Birmingham Jail. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about civil disobedience, just vs. unjust laws, and King's argument against gradualism. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Letter from Birmingham Jail in more depth. Cover King's distinction between just and unjust laws, his defense of nonviolent direct action, his rejection of gradualism, and how he grounds his argument in natural-rights philosophy from the Declaration of Independence.

items.title: Marbury v. Madison
items.byline: Established judicial review — the Supreme Court's power to strike down laws that violate the Constitution
items.key_takeaway: The Supreme Court can declare acts of Congress unconstitutional, making the judiciary a co-equal branch with the power to interpret the Constitution.
items.summary: William Marbury sued for a judicial commission withheld by the new Jefferson administration. Chief Justice John Marshall ruled that Marbury had a right to the commission, but the Court could not order its delivery because the law granting that power — Section 13 of the Judiciary Act of 1789 — unconstitutionally expanded the Court's original jurisdiction beyond what Article III allowed. By striking down part of an act of Congress, Marshall established judicial review: the Court's authority to void laws that conflict with the Constitution. This single ruling transformed the judiciary into a true co-equal branch.
items.constitutional_link: Article III · Article VI Supremacy Clause
items.exam_appearance: Nearly every unit tests this · MCQs on branch conflict · FRQ precedent citations

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Marbury v. Madison. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about judicial review, the facts of the case, and its impact on the judiciary. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Marbury v. Madison in more depth. Cover the political context of the midnight appointments, John Marshall's reasoning, how it established judicial review, and why it made the Supreme Court a co-equal branch.

items.title: McCulloch v. Maryland
items.byline: Upheld Congress's implied powers and established federal supremacy over state law
items.key_takeaway: Congress has implied powers under the Necessary and Proper Clause, and states cannot tax or interfere with legitimate federal institutions under the Supremacy Clause.
items.summary: Maryland tried to tax the Second Bank of the United States to drive it out of the state. Chief Justice Marshall ruled: (1) Congress had the power to charter the bank under the Necessary and Proper Clause, even though 'bank' isn't in the enumerated powers — any means rationally related to an enumerated power is constitutional. (2) Maryland couldn't tax the bank because 'the power to tax is the power to destroy,' and under the Supremacy Clause, states cannot impair federal institutions. This case is the exam's canonical example of expansive federal power and the implied/elastic clause doctrine.
items.constitutional_link: Article I Section 8 (Necessary and Proper) · Article VI (Supremacy)
items.exam_appearance: Federalism FRQs · MCQs on implied powers and state–federal conflict

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on McCulloch v. Maryland. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about implied powers, the Necessary and Proper Clause, and the Supremacy Clause. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain McCulloch v. Maryland in more depth. Cover the Second Bank controversy, Marshall's reasoning on implied powers, the Necessary and Proper Clause, and how it established federal supremacy over state taxation of federal institutions.

items.title: Schenck v. United States
items.byline: Established the 'clear and present danger' test limiting free speech in wartime
items.key_takeaway: Free speech is not absolute — speech that creates a 'clear and present danger' of producing harms Congress can prevent is not protected by the First Amendment.
items.summary: Schenck mailed leaflets urging draft resistance during WWI and was convicted under the Espionage Act. The Supreme Court upheld the conviction. Justice Holmes wrote that free speech does not protect someone 'falsely shouting fire in a crowded theater' — speech creating a clear and present danger of substantive evils Congress can prevent falls outside the First Amendment. Though Schenck's specific test has been largely replaced by the Brandenburg 'imminent lawless action' test (1969), it is still a required case because it established that First Amendment protections are not unlimited, especially in national security contexts.

paired_with.title: New York Times Co. v. United States

items.exam_appearance: Civil liberties FRQs · MCQs on free-speech limits

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Schenck v. United States. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about the clear and present danger test, the First Amendment limits, and the case facts. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Schenck v. United States in more depth. Cover the WWI context, Holmes's 'fire in a crowded theater' analogy, the clear and present danger test, and how later cases (like Tinker and Brandenburg) have modified its reach.

items.title: Brown v. Board of Education
items.byline: Declared racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional, overturning 'separate but equal'
items.key_takeaway: Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal and violate the 14th Amendment's Equal Protection Clause — overturning Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine.
items.summary: The Court unanimously struck down racial segregation in public schools, rejecting the 'separate but equal' doctrine established by Plessy v. Ferguson (1896). Chief Justice Warren's opinion argued that segregation generates feelings of inferiority in Black children that damage their education in ways that cannot be undone by equal facilities — 'separate educational facilities are inherently unequal.' The ruling relied on the Equal Protection Clause of the 14th Amendment and social-science evidence. It catalyzed the civil rights movement and established the modern framework for equal-protection analysis.
items.exam_appearance: Civil rights FRQs · MCQs on equal protection · constant precedent citation

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Brown v. Board of Education. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about the Equal Protection Clause, the overturning of Plessy, and the case's impact. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Brown v. Board of Education in more depth. Cover the Plessy v. Ferguson 'separate but equal' doctrine it overturned, Chief Justice Warren's reasoning, the role of the Equal Protection Clause, and how it launched the modern civil rights movement.

items.title: Baker v. Carr
items.byline: Ruled federal courts can hear redistricting cases, establishing the 'one person, one vote' principle
items.key_takeaway: Redistricting and apportionment are justiciable under the Equal Protection Clause — federal courts can order states to redraw unequal legislative districts.
items.summary: Tennessee had not redrawn its state legislative districts in over 60 years, resulting in rural districts with far fewer voters than urban ones. The Court held that federal courts have jurisdiction over such redistricting cases — they are not 'political questions' outside judicial review. Although this case only opened the door, it led directly to Reynolds v. Sims (1964) and the 'one person, one vote' principle requiring roughly equal population in legislative districts. Baker v. Carr is the exam's key case for the justiciability of redistricting and voter equality under the 14th Amendment.
items.exam_appearance: Electoral process FRQs · MCQs on redistricting and apportionment

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Baker v. Carr. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about justiciability, redistricting, and 'one person, one vote.' Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Baker v. Carr in more depth. Cover Tennessee's malapportionment problem, why the case established justiciability of redistricting, how it led to 'one person, one vote,' and how it compares to Shaw v. Reno.

items.title: Engel v. Vitale
items.byline: Ruled that state-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause
items.key_takeaway: Government-written or government-mandated prayer in public schools violates the First Amendment's Establishment Clause, even if the prayer is nondenominational and participation is voluntary.
items.summary: New York's Board of Regents composed a short, nondenominational prayer recited daily in public schools. The Court held this violated the Establishment Clause of the First Amendment. Voluntariness did not save the policy — any official government prayer in public schools unconstitutionally entangles government with religion. The Establishment Clause's purpose is to ensure no government sponsorship of religion, even mild or optional sponsorship. This case, along with Abington v. Schempp (1963), broadly ended official religious activities in public schools.
items.exam_appearance: Civil liberties FRQs · MCQs on Establishment Clause

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Engel v. Vitale. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about the Establishment Clause, state-sponsored prayer, and the case holding. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Engel v. Vitale in more depth. Cover the New York Regents' prayer, the Establishment Clause reasoning, why voluntariness didn't matter, and how it compares to free-exercise cases like Wisconsin v. Yoder.

items.title: Gideon v. Wainwright
items.byline: Incorporated the 6th Amendment right to counsel to the states through the 14th Amendment
items.key_takeaway: States must provide attorneys to criminal defendants who cannot afford one — the 6th Amendment right to counsel applies to state courts via the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause (selective incorporation).
items.summary: Clarence Earl Gideon was charged with a felony in Florida, couldn't afford a lawyer, and was denied one by the state court. He was convicted. The Supreme Court unanimously ruled that the 6th Amendment right to counsel is fundamental to a fair trial and applies to the states through the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause — a process called selective incorporation. States must now provide public defenders to indigent defendants facing serious criminal charges. This case is the exam's primary example of selective incorporation at work.
items.exam_appearance: Civil liberties FRQs · MCQs on selective incorporation

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Gideon v. Wainwright. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about the right to counsel, selective incorporation, and the 14th Amendment. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Gideon v. Wainwright in more depth. Cover the facts of Gideon's case, the 6th Amendment right to counsel, how selective incorporation works, and how it compares to McDonald v. Chicago as an incorporation case.

items.title: Tinker v. Des Moines
items.byline: Students and teachers do not 'shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate'
items.key_takeaway: Students retain First Amendment rights in public schools — symbolic speech can only be restricted if it substantially disrupts the educational environment.
items.summary: Students wore black armbands to protest the Vietnam War and were suspended. The Supreme Court ruled that wearing armbands was symbolic speech protected by the First Amendment. Famously, 'students do not shed their constitutional rights to freedom of speech or expression at the schoolhouse gate.' Schools can only restrict student expression when it materially and substantially interferes with school operations or the rights of others. This 'substantial disruption' test remains the standard for student free speech cases, though later cases (e.g., Morse v. Frederick, Hazelwood) have carved out exceptions.
items.exam_appearance: Civil liberties FRQs · MCQs on symbolic speech and student rights

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Tinker v. Des Moines. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about symbolic speech, the substantial disruption test, and student First Amendment rights. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Tinker v. Des Moines in more depth. Cover the armband protest, symbolic speech, the 'substantial disruption' test, the 'schoolhouse gate' quote, and how it compares to Schenck as a free-speech case.

items.title: New York Times Co. v. United States
items.byline: Blocked the government from prior restraint of the press publishing the Pentagon Papers
items.key_takeaway: The government bears a heavy burden to justify prior restraint on the press, and national security claims alone are not enough to block publication under the First Amendment.
items.summary: The Nixon administration tried to prevent the New York Times and Washington Post from publishing the Pentagon Papers — a classified study exposing government deception about the Vietnam War. The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the government had not met the 'heavy presumption against prior restraint.' The First Amendment's guarantee of a free press is strongest against prior restraint; vague national-security claims don't justify blocking publication. This is the exam's canonical free-press case and a limit on executive power.
items.exam_appearance: Civil liberties FRQs · MCQs on press freedom and prior restraint

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on New York Times Co. v. United States. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about prior restraint, the Pentagon Papers, and freedom of the press. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain New York Times Co. v. United States in more depth. Cover the Pentagon Papers, the Nixon administration's claim, what prior restraint means, why the government failed to meet its burden, and how it compares to Schenck v. United States.

items.title: Wisconsin v. Yoder
items.byline: Ruled Amish families could withdraw children from school after 8th grade on Free Exercise grounds
items.key_takeaway: The Free Exercise Clause protects sincere religious practices from government interference, even when they conflict with otherwise valid laws — compelling state interests must meet strict scrutiny.
items.summary: Wisconsin required all children to attend school until age 16. Amish parents refused to send their children to high school, arguing it would endanger their religious community's way of life. The Supreme Court unanimously sided with the Amish, holding that the state's compulsory-attendance law violated the Free Exercise Clause. The state's interest in education did not override the Amish's sincere religious practice under strict scrutiny. The case is the exam's key example of free-exercise protection and religious accommodation.
items.exam_appearance: Civil liberties FRQs · MCQs on free exercise and religious accommodation

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Wisconsin v. Yoder. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about the Free Exercise Clause, compulsory attendance, and religious accommodation. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Wisconsin v. Yoder in more depth. Cover the Amish community's religious concerns, the compulsory-attendance law, strict scrutiny analysis, and how it compares to Engel v. Vitale as an Establishment Clause case.

items.title: Shaw v. Reno
items.byline: Ruled racial gerrymandering violates the Equal Protection Clause even when designed to help minorities
items.key_takeaway: Legislative districts drawn primarily on the basis of race are subject to strict scrutiny under the Equal Protection Clause — even 'majority-minority' districts must have a compelling justification.
items.summary: North Carolina created a bizarrely shaped majority-Black congressional district. The Court ruled that districts drawn with race as the predominant factor violate the 14th Amendment's Equal Protection Clause unless they survive strict scrutiny. Race-based districting — even well-intentioned, to increase minority representation — receives the same skepticism as any racial classification. The case is the exam's key limit on racial gerrymandering and complements Baker v. Carr on the justiciability of redistricting.
items.exam_appearance: Electoral process FRQs · MCQs on gerrymandering and equal protection

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Shaw v. Reno. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about racial gerrymandering, the Equal Protection Clause, and strict scrutiny. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Shaw v. Reno in more depth. Cover North Carolina's redistricting, racial gerrymandering, strict scrutiny analysis, and how it compares to Baker v. Carr on redistricting.

items.title: United States v. Lopez
items.byline: Limited Congress's Commerce Clause power by striking down the Gun-Free School Zones Act
items.key_takeaway: Congress's power under the Commerce Clause has limits — it cannot regulate activities with only tenuous economic connections, reinforcing state sovereignty over purely local matters.
items.summary: The Gun-Free School Zones Act made it a federal crime to bring a gun near a school. The Court struck it down, ruling Congress had exceeded its Commerce Clause authority — gun possession near schools is not economic activity that substantially affects interstate commerce. The ruling reaffirmed federalism by insisting the federal government has enumerated, not unlimited, powers. Lopez is the exam's primary case for limits on federal power and contrasts with McCulloch v. Maryland's broad reading of federal authority.
items.constitutional_link: Article I Section 8 · Commerce Clause · 10th Amendment
items.exam_appearance: Federalism FRQs · MCQs on Commerce Clause limits

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on United States v. Lopez. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about the Commerce Clause, limits on federal power, and federalism. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain United States v. Lopez in more depth. Cover the Gun-Free School Zones Act, the Commerce Clause analysis, the 10th Amendment implications, and how it contrasts with McCulloch v. Maryland.

items.title: McDonald v. Chicago
items.byline: Incorporated the 2nd Amendment right to bear arms to the states through the 14th Amendment
items.key_takeaway: The 2nd Amendment right to keep and bear arms is fundamental and applies to the states through the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause (selective incorporation).
items.summary: Chicago had strict handgun restrictions. After DC v. Heller (2008) affirmed an individual right to bear arms at the federal level, McDonald asked whether the 2nd Amendment also binds the states. The Court ruled yes — through selective incorporation via the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause, the 2nd Amendment applies to state and local governments. This case is the exam's canonical modern incorporation case and directly parallels Gideon v. Wainwright's logic for the 6th Amendment.
items.exam_appearance: Civil liberties FRQs · MCQs on selective incorporation

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on McDonald v. Chicago. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about the 2nd Amendment, selective incorporation, and the 14th Amendment. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain McDonald v. Chicago in more depth. Cover DC v. Heller's ruling, the question of incorporation, the 14th Amendment Due Process analysis, and how it parallels Gideon v. Wainwright.

items.title: Citizens United v. FEC
items.byline: Ruled corporations and unions have a First Amendment right to unlimited independent political expenditures
items.key_takeaway: The First Amendment protects political speech regardless of the speaker's identity — so government cannot restrict independent campaign expenditures by corporations or unions.
items.summary: Citizens United, a nonprofit corporation, was barred by the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA) from airing a political film near an election. The Court struck down the restriction, holding that political speech is protected by the First Amendment regardless of whether the speaker is an individual or a corporation. This opened the door to super PACs, which can raise and spend unlimited money on independent political expenditures (though not coordinated with or contributed directly to candidates). The case is central to AP Gov debates about campaign finance, corporate speech, and political inequality.
items.exam_appearance: Electoral process FRQs · MCQs on campaign finance and free speech

adi_prompts.quiz: Quiz me on Citizens United v. FEC. Give me 4 AP-style multiple choice questions one at a time about campaign finance, corporate speech, and the First Amendment. Wait for my answer before explaining.
adi_prompts.explain: Explain Citizens United v. FEC in more depth. Cover the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act, the corporate-speech argument, the distinction between independent expenditures and direct contributions, and the rise of super PACs.
